subject: Chemistry
title: Xenon: The Stranger Gas
description: A micro-lesson that makes Xenon: The Stranger Gas usable.

hook.text: Xenon means 'stranger' in Greek. For decades, scientists believed it could never bond with anything.

buildup.text: As a noble gas, xenon has a full outer shell. The textbooks said noble gases were permanently inert.

discovery.text: In 1962, Neil Bartlett forced xenon to react with platinum fluoride—creating the first noble gas compound.

twist.text: Textbooks worldwide had to be rewritten overnight. 'Noble' didn't mean 'impossible.'

climax.text: Xenon compounds now exist with fluorine and oxygen. Even the laziest elements can be forced to react.

punchline.text: The 'stranger' broke the rules. Noble gases can form bonds.

quiz.question: What did Neil Bartlett prove about noble gases?
options: That they can form chemical compounds under the right conditions
That they are the most common gases in the atmosphere
